comments:
Summary:
This paper proposes applying optimistic concurrency control (OCC) to distributed unsupervised learning problems (clustering and latent feature models). It presents OCC-based distributed variants of DP-Means, BP-Means and an online facility location (OFL) algorithm. The authors prove serializability of the distributed algorithms, show approximation guarantees for the OFL/DP-means linkage, and derive a bound on the expected number of serial validations (rejections) for DP-Means under a separability assumption. They provide simulations and Spark/EC2 experiments demonstrating scaling behavior.

Strengths:
- Clear and well-motivated idea: adapting OCC from databases to distributed ML is a natural and useful middle ground between strict locking and fully coordination-free approaches.
- Nontrivial theoretical contributions: (i) serializability proofs for the distributed versions, (ii) connection between DP-Means and facility location enabling approximation bounds, and (iii) an analytic bound (Theorem 3.3) on master-validated proposals under a cluster separation model.
- Practical implementation and empirical evaluation: the authors implement algorithms in Spark, run large-scale experiments, and report scaling behavior across multiple settings.
- Pseudocode and proof sketches are provided, increasing reproducibility and clarity of the core ideas.

Weaknesses / Points for improvement:
- Strong assumptions in some analyses. The scalability bound (Thm. 3.3) depends on a well-separated cluster model (cluster diameter and inter-cluster distance bounded by λ). While the authors acknowledge this and present empirical results suggesting wider applicability, a more detailed discussion of how results degrade when clusters overlap would strengthen the paper.
- Experiments are largely synthetic. The paper would benefit from experiments on real-world datasets (with varying cluster overlap and higher-dimensional structure) to validate practicality and robustness.
- Master bottleneck / validation cost. Although the theoretical bound shows dependence on Pb and k_N rather than N, in practice the master validation step may become a runtime bottleneck when many proposals occur early (authors observe no scaling in early epochs). More detailed profiling (time spent in validation, network costs, master CPU/memory behavior) and comparisons to coordination-free baselines would be useful.
- Asynchrony and fault tolerance. The paper adopts bulk-synchronous epochs for simplicity, but distributed systems often operate asynchronously. More discussion (and ideally experiment) on asynchronous OCC variants, stale-replica handling, and failure modes would increase practical applicability.
- BP-means update cost. For feature re-estimation the paper uses an (Z^T Z)^{-1} computation; when number of features grows this could be expensive or ill-conditioned. Some discussion of numerical stability, regularization, or incremental updates would help.
- Comparisons to competing distributed strategies. While related work is discussed, stronger empirical baselines (e.g., coordination-free implementations, lock-based variants, or divide-and-conquer approaches) would more clearly quantify the tradeoffs in speed vs. correctness overhead.

Clarity / Presentation:
- Overall the paper is well written and organized; pseudocode and appendices with proofs are helpful. A reader would appreciate more implementation details (how the master serializes proposals in Spark, communication patterns, and bootstrapping effect) and a more explicit statement of typical costs per epoch.

Recommendation / Suggested experiments:
- Add experiments on several real datasets with different cluster/feature overlap to test robustness.
- Profile master validation costs and network overhead to show when OCC yields practical speedups and when the master becomes limiting.
- Provide empirical comparisons to at least one coordination-free competitor and one locking-based approach to highlight practical tradeoffs.
- Discuss or experiment with asynchronous execution variants and how OCC would be implemented beyond the bulk-synchronous model.

Overall assessment:
This is a solid contribution that brings a principled concurrency-control view to distributed unsupervised learning. The theoretical results are sound under stated assumptions and the practical experiments are encouraging. With broader empirical validation and more discussion of practical bottlenecks, the work would be of high interest to the community.
